rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical intramolecular aminoazidation of 2-vinylanilines (N-Ts) with NaN3 to give 3-azido-indolines via an iodonium-ion intermediate
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of iodide (I-) to molecular I2, which then forms an iodonium ion with the styrenyl alkene; the cathode reduces protons to H2

key_intermediates.name: molecular iodine (I2) generated at anode from I-
cyclized indolinium cation C (after intramolecular N-attack)
3-azido-indoline product 3a (after SN2 by azide on D)

electron_flow: Anode oxidizes I- to I2; I2 adds to the styrene alkene of 1a forming an iodonium ion, which is opened by intramolecular nucleophilic attack of the tosylamide nitrogen to give a 3-iodo-indoline after deprotonation; azide then displaces iodide via SN2 to give the 3-azido-indoline, while the cathode reduces protons to H2.
rate_determining_step: Slow electrochemical generation of I2 from I- (matching the rate of iodonium formation with intramolecular amine cyclization to suppress competing direct amine addition)

probe_reagents.name: BHT (2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-methylphenol)
probe_reagents.observation: Addition of BHT did not decrease the yield (3a obtained in 72%), indicating no radical intermediate is involved

probe_reagents.name: I2 (stoichiometric, no electricity)
probe_reagents.observation: 1 equiv I2 with HOAc in CH3CN:H2O at 25 °C gave 3a in 47%, supporting I2 as the active species but showing direct I2 addition is less efficient than slow electrogeneration

notes: Authors argue that under direct I2 catalysis, rapid iodonium formation outpaces intramolecular amine cyclization (aniline competes via nucleophilic addition first), lowering yield; slow electrogeneration of I2 from I- matches the cyclization rate and suppresses this side pathway. No radical pathway is supported by the BHT experiment.
